evidence_quotes.quote: Analogously, with digital products such as video games that can be disabled by companies at any time, for any or no reason, this is abusing and disrespecting the rights of the consumer to an intolerable degree that must be rectified.

Publishers disable and destroy access to former game titles as a form of planned obsolescence that forces customers to continually buy newer, more expensive games, when artistically these newer games cannot be considered replacements for the original.

cloud security checks and Digital Rights Management disabling what customers purchase is a natural evolution of commerce, rather than a violation of existing rules, understandings, and regulations on goods and services and exploiting grey areas in commerce law to maximize profit.
hostile license agreements that likely violate European Directives such as 93/13/EEC and 2011/83/EU

paragraphs.text: I am speaking as someone who has been with the Stop Killing Games movement since its more formal inception in early 2024. Although I am American, I am intending on applying for European citizenship at some point in the future, and I admire the European Unions efforts on consumer protection. My experiences with this movement have educated me greatly on the rights of customers and considerations of goods vs services in the Union. With that in mind, I hope you consider my words despite my lack of European citizenship. The digital spaces we inhabit, which most of us are part of or leave a digital footprint in, have been subjected to a form of "enshittification" as defined by Cory Doctorow: Corporations are increasingly dictating access to our digital spaces and platforms, and employing the use of digital products with terms and conditions that are of questionable legality at best (within the European Union) such that they disempower customers and users of digital spaces from being able to enjoy what they paid for. This is the process by which digital platforms die: Stage 1: Good for Users - The platform is initially beneficial to its users. Stage 2: Abusing Users for Business Customers - The platform then leverages its users to provide better services or value to its business customers. Stage 3: Abusing Business Customers for Self-Benefit - The platform exploits its business customers to reclaim all the value for itself, leading to its eventual decline and "death" Analogously, with digital products such as video games that can be disabled by companies at any time, for any or no reason, this is abusing and disrespecting the rights of the consumer to an intolerable degree that must be rectified. Profit for the sake of growing profits cannot disregard customers of any industry, let alone an industry worth more than film and music combined. Such a mindset supports planned obsolescence and a "cancer mentality" of extracting value from others to enrich oneself. Publishers disable and destroy access to former game titles as a form of planned obsolescence that forces customers to continually buy newer, more expensive games, when artistically these newer games cannot be considered replacements for the original. The overall situation is becoming like a form of corporate digital feudalism, where customers are being regarded increasingly as like animals with no rights to own and use what they pay money for, especially in the United States, with hostile license agreements that likely violate European Directives such as 93/13/EEC and 2011/83/EU and an ignorance of the Brussels Effect contributing to a sentiment among customers that nothing can be done to challenge these corporations to the benefit of customers, that cloud security checks and Digital Rights Management disabling what customers purchase is a natural evolution of commerce, rather than a violation of existing rules, understandings, and regulations on goods and services and exploiting grey areas in commerce law to maximize profit. Not to mention that voting with your wallet is becoming untenable if all products on the market are transitioning to contain online dependencies and killswitches that companies can activate whenever products become unprofitable. Government regulation on this is needed. As such, I write this comment enthusiastically supporting the aims of the Digital Fairness Act, and hope that the Union can consider protection for video games and other digital products that experience planned obsolescence, such as cars (Fiskers Ocean vehicles and Kevin Mulligan, BMWs heated car seats, Mercedes charging for acceleration increases in EQ cars), brain implants (NeuroVista and Rita Leggett - paper attached), bionic eyes (Second Sight Medical Products), trains (Polish train manufacturer NewAg), dishwashers (Bosch 500 series), headsets (Magic Leap), education tools (eduVULCAN in Poland), thermostats and other smart home devices, and Spotifys Car Thing.
